Journal.Title: FASEB journal : official publication of the Federation of American Societies for Experimental Biology

Article.ArticleTitle: Maf1 Ameliorates Cardiac Fibrosis by Methylation of the Sfrp2 Promoter Through Dnmt1.

Abstract.AbstractText: Cardiac fibroblast (CF) differentiation into myofibroblasts is a crucial driver of cardiac fibrosis, leading to myocardial stiffness and eventually impairing heart function. Cardiomyocyte-fibroblast intercellular communication has emerged as a key regulatory way for CF activation and the fibrotic response. However, the molecular mechanisms linking cardiomyocyte secretomes to CF activation within heart failure remain poorly understood. Here, we identified a stress-responsive protein Maf1 in cardiomyocytes as a central regulator of CF activation in both in vivo and in vitro models of cardiac fibrosis. Maf1 overexpression (cardiomyocyte-specific Maf1 overexpression mice, Maf1 cOE) attenuated CF proliferation, ECM protein expression, and myofibroblast differentiation, while Maf1 loss-of-function (Maf1 knockout mice, Maf1-KO) exacerbated cardiac fibrosis. Notably, Maf1 directly suppresses the expression and secretion of Sfrp2 by affecting its promoter DNA methylation through DNA methyltransferase 1 (Dnmt1), which is essential for promoting CF activation and fibrosis. Sfrp2 overexpression or Sfrp2 recombinant protein treatment exacerbates TGFβ1-induced fibrosis, while silencing Dnmt1 reverses the upregulation of Sfrp2 by Maf1. These findings identify Maf1 as a pivotal link between cardiomyocyte secretomes and fibrosis, suggesting it as a potential therapeutic target to mitigate fibrosis and enhance cardiac recovery during heart failure.
Abstract.CopyrightInformation: © 2026 Federation of American Societies for Experimental Biology.

T. Aoki, Y. Fukumoto, K. Sugimura, et al., “Prognostic Impact of Myocardial Interstitial Fibrosis in Non‐Ischemic Heart Failure ‐ Comparison Between Preserved and Reduced Ejection Fraction Heart Failure ‐: – Comparison Between Preserved and Reduced Ejection Fraction Heart Failure,” Circulation Journal 75 (2011): 2605–2613.
M. Liu, B. de López Juan Abad, and K. Cheng, “Cardiac Fibrosis: Myofibroblast‐Mediated Pathological Regulation and Drug Delivery Strategies,” Advanced Drug Delivery Reviews 173 (2021): 504–519.
I. Valiente‐Alandi, A. E. Schafer, and B. C. Blaxall, “Extracellular Matrix‐Mediated Cellular Communication in the Heart,” Journal of Molecular and Cellular Cardiology 91 (2016): 228–237.

N. Hermida, L. Michel, H. Esfahani, et al., “Cardiac Myocyte β3‐Adrenergic Receptors Prevent Myocardial Fibrosis by Modulating Oxidant Stress‐Dependent Paracrine Signaling,” European Heart Journal 39 (2018): 888–898.

I. M. Willis, “Maf1 Phenotypes and Cell Physiology,” Biochimica et Biophysica Acta (BBA) ‐ Gene Regulatory Mechanisms 1861 (2018): 330–337.
R. D. Moir, J. Lee, and I. M. Willis, “Recovery of RNA Polymerase III Transcription From the Glycerol‐Repressed State,” Journal of Biological Chemistry 287 (2012): 30833–30841.

S. Zhang, X. Li, H.‐Y. Wang, and X. F. Steven Zheng, “Beyond Regulation of Pol III: Role of MAF1 in Growth, Metabolism, Aging and Cancer,” Biochimica et Biophysica Acta (BBA) ‐ Gene Regulatory Mechanisms 1861 (2018): 338–343.

Y. Dai, M. Yan, J. Wan, and T. Xiao, “Maf1 Mitigates Sevoflurane‐Induced Microglial Inflammatory Damage and Attenuates Microglia‐Mediated Neurotoxicity in HT‐22 Cells by Activating the AMPK/Nrf2 Signaling,” Neurotoxicology 90 (2022): 237–245.

X. Wei, W. Jiang, Z. Wang, et al., “Feedback Loop Centered on MAF1 Reduces Blood‐Brain Barrier Damage in Sepsis‐Associated Encephalopathy,” Cellular & Molecular Biology Letters 30 (2025): 8.
P. J. Thul, L. Åkesson, M. Wiking, et al., “A Subcellular Map of the Human Proteome,” Science 356 (2017): eaal3321.
M. Karlsson, C. Zhang, L. Méar, et al., “A Single‐Cell Type Transcriptomics Map of Human Tissues,” Science Advances 7 (2021): eabh2169.

Y. Sun, W. Fan, R. Xue, et al., “Transcribed Ultraconserved Regions, uc.323, Ameliorates Cardiac Hypertrophy by Regulating the Transcription of CPT1b (Carnitine Palmitoyl Transferase 1b),” Hypertension 75 (2020): 79–90.
M. Mastri, Z. Shah, K. Hsieh, et al., “Secreted Frizzled‐Related Protein 2 as a Target in Antifibrotic Therapeutic Intervention,” American Journal of Physiology‐Cell Physiology 306 (2014): C531–C539.

K. Kobayashi, M. Luo, Y. Zhang, et al., “Secreted Frizzled‐Related Protein 2 Is a Procollagen C Proteinase Enhancer With a Role in Fibrosis Associated With Myocardial Infarction,” Nature Cell Biology 11 (2009): 46–55.

A. Gerber, K. Ito, C.‐S. Chu, and R. G. Roeder, “Gene‐Specific Control of tRNA Expression by RNA Polymerase II,” Molecular Cell 78 (2020): 765–778.e7.
A. Khanna, A. Pradhan, and S. P. Curran, “Emerging Roles for Maf1 Beyond the Regulation of RNA Polymerase III Activity,” Journal of Molecular Biology 427 (2015): 2577–2585.
I. M. Willis and R. D. Moir, “Signaling to and From the RNA Polymerase III Transcription and Processing Machinery,” Annual Review of Biochemistry 87 (2018): 75–100.
Y. Kulaberoglu, Y. Malik, G. Borland, C. Selman, N. Alic, and J. M. A. Tullet, “RNA Polymerase III, Ageing and Longevity,” Frontiers in Genetics 12 (2021): 705122.

Y. Li, C. K. Tsang, S. Wang, et al., “MAF1 Suppresses AKT‐mTOR Signaling and Liver Cancer Through Activation of PTEN Transcription,” Hepatology 63 (2016): 1928–1942.

H. Lin, M. Angeli, K. J. Chung, C. Ejimadu, A. R. Rosa, and T. Lee, “sFRP2 Activates Wnt/β‐Catenin Signaling in Cardiac Fibroblasts: Differential Roles in Cell Growth, Energy Metabolism, and Extracellular Matrix Remodeling,” American Journal of Physiology‐Cell Physiology 311 (2016): C710–C719.
H. Tao, J.‐J. Yang, K.‐H. Shi, Z.‐Y. Deng, and J. Li, “DNA Methylation in Cardiac Fibrosis: New Advances and Perspectives,” Toxicology 323 (2014): 125–129.
T. Xue, X. Qiu, H. Liu, et al., “Epigenetic Regulation in Fibrosis Progress,” Pharmacological Research 173 (2021): 105910.
R. Papait, S. Serio, and G. Condorelli, “Role of the Epigenome in Heart Failure,” Physiological Reviews 100 (2020): 1753–1777.

S. A. Brodie, G. Li, A. El‐Kommos, et al., “Class I HDACs Are Mediators of Smoke Carcinogen‐Induced Stabilization of DNMT1 and Serve as Promising Targets for Chemoprevention of Lung Cancer,” Cancer Prevention Research (Philadelphia, Pa.) 7 (2014): 351–361.

N. M. Samaei, Y. Yazdani, R. Alizadeh‐Navaei, H. Azadeh, and T. Farazmandfar, “Promoter Methylation Analysis of WNT/β‐Catenin Pathway Regulators and Its Association With Expression of DNMT1 Enzyme in Colorectal Cancer,” Journal of Biomedical Science 21 (2014): 73.

Y. Liu, Z. Sun, D. Xu, et al., “Hesperidin Derivative‐11 Inhibits Fibroblast‐Like Synoviocytes Proliferation by Activating Secreted Frizzled‐Related Protein 2 in Adjuvant Arthritis Rats,” European Journal of Pharmacology 794 (2017): 173–183.

C. Miao, J. Chang, J. Dou, Y. Xiong, and G. Zhou, “DNA Hypermethylation of SFRP2 Influences the Pathology of Rheumatoid Arthritis Through the Canonical Wnt Signaling in Model Rats,” Autoimmunity 51 (2018): 1–14.

M. Yang, L. Li, X. Huang, H. Xing, L. Hong, and C. Jiang, “The DNA Demethylation‐Regulated SFRP2 Dictates the Progression of Endometriosis via Activation of the Wnt/β‐Catenin Signaling Pathway,” BMC Molecular and Cell Biology 24 (2023): 12.
